elements.sent-2411-001: Tiepolo 11,34143 Trieste, Italy \and Universidad Andres Bello, Facultad de Ciencias Exactas, Departamento de Ciencias F{\'\i sicas - Instituto de Astrof{\'\i sica, Autopista Concepci{\'o n-Talcahuano, 7100, Talcahuano, Chile \and Leibniz-Institut f\"ur Astrophysik Potsdam, An der Sternwarte 16, 14482 Potsdam, Germany \and European Southern Observatory, Alonso de Cordova 3107, Vitacura, Santiago, Chile \and GEPI, Observatoire de Paris, Universit\'{e PSL, CNRS, 77 Av.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-002: Dendert-Rocheau, 75014 Paris, France \and UPJV, Universit\'e de Picardie Jules Verne, 33 rue St Leu, 80080 Amiens, France \and Landessternwarte - Zentrum f\"ur Astronomie der Universit\"at Heidelberg, K\"onigstuhl 12, 69117 Heidelberg, Germany \and Institute of Fundamental Physics of the Universe, Via Beirut 2,34151 Trieste, Italy \abstract {The small- to intermediate-mass ($\rm M<0.8 M_\odot$), most metal-poor stars that formed in the infancy of the Universe are still shining today in the sky.}
elements.sent-2411-003: They are very rare, but their discovery and investigation brings new knowledge on the formation of the first stellar generations.
elements.sent-2411-004: Since no carbon can be detected in its spectrum, a careful upper limit is important, both to classify this star and to distinguish it from the carbon-enhanced stars that represent the majority at these metallicities.
elements.sent-2411-005: The new spectra were combined with archival spectra in order to increase the signal-to-noise ratio.
elements.sent-2411-006: From the combined spectrum, we derived abundances for seven elements (Mg, Si, Ca, Ti, Fe, Ni, and a tentative Li) and five significant upper limits (C, Na, Al, Sr, and Ba).
elements.sent-2411-007: A feature compatible with the Li doublet at 670.7\,nm is tentatively detected.
elements.sent-2411-008: Therefore, the gas cloud out of which the star was formed did not cool via atomic lines but probably through dust.
elements.sent-2411-009: Fragmentation of the primordial cloud is another possibility for the formation of a star with a metallicity this low.. \nolinenumbers.
elements.sent-2411-010: The process of formation of the first generation of stars (Pop\,III), formed after the Big Bang, is still not clearly understood and remains to be identified \citep[see][]{bromm13 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: One of the main questions is whether low-mass stars can form from the primordial material, composed of isotopes of H, He, and traces of Li.
elements.sent-2411-012: A star of mass $M\approx0.8\,{\rm M _\odot$ or lower that formed in the first 100 Myr after the Big Bang would still be shining today on the main sequence.}
elements.sent-2411-013: The fact that, to date, such a star has not been observed could serve as an argument against the formation of such primordial stars, yet the absence of evidence is not evidence of absence.
elements.sent-2411-014: Theoretically, the difficulty of forming a low-mass star from primordial matter is due to the lack of an efficient cooling mechanism that allows the star-forming gas cloud to continue to collapse instead of being stopped by the increasing radiative pressure caused by the consequential heating from compression.
elements.sent-2411-015: A star of large mass can be formed, since the gas cloud's gravity is strong enough to overcome the pressure resulting from this heating \citep[see ][and references therein]{klessen23 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: However, the simulation could not be run long enough to be sure that the fragments will not merge again giving rise to a single massive star.
elements.sent-2411-017: This led \citet{frebel07 to introduce the transition discriminant $\rm D=\log\left [ 10^{[C/H] +0.3\times10^{[O/H]}\right ]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: The stars formed through atomic line cooling are characterised by $\rm D \ge D_{crit}\simeq -3.5\pm 0.2$.
elements.sent-2411-019: The presence of carbon and oxygen in a primordial gas cloud provides a means of cooling the contracting gas to allow the formation of low-mass stars.
elements.sent-2411-020: But there is another way to cool at a chemical composition close to the pristine one.
elements.sent-2411-021: The star \leol\ (also known as {\it Gaia}\,DR3\,3890626773968983296, hereafter \leo) was first studied by \citet{leo11 who claimed, based on UVES spectra, a very low iron abundance, $\rm [Fe/H]=-4.73$, and a very low carbon abundance upper limit, $\rm A(C) \le 4.7$.}
elements.sent-2411-022: These authors found that the transition discriminant in this star is below the critical value defined above, thus invoking the cooling through dust \citep{omukai05 to explain the formation of this star.}
elements.sent-2411-023: The formation of \leo\ was theoretically investigated by several authors, and the conclusion was that the cooling was due to dust \citep[see][]{schneider12,klessen12,chiaki14,bovino16 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Clearly at the time of the discovery of \leo, there was no way to derive an accurate surface gravity; the photometry available was consistent not only for a star on the main sequence, but also for a turn-off or sub-giant branch star.
elements.sent-2411-025: Their explanation for the low metal abundance of this star was that it is the result of 'gravitational settling on the main sequence followed by incomplete convective dredge-up during subgiant evolution'.
elements.sent-2411-026: In this scenario, the fact that Fe is enhanced with respect to C implied that it was formed in an environment enriched by Type Ia supernova ejecta rich in Fe.
elements.sent-2411-027: With the advent of {\it Gaia , the parallax put the star on the main sequence \citep[see][]{leo18 .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Furthermore, the investigation of Ca ionisation conducted by \citet{sitnova19 , which took into account the effects due to departures from the local thermodynamical equilibrium (LTE), that is so-called non-LTE (NLTE) effects.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Recently, a more sophisticated analysis of the same UVES data used in \citet{leo11 , by \citet{lagae23 , using a 3D stellar model and a NLTE treatment of the line formation for some elements, claimed that these new assumptions implied that the star was not as iron poor as was suggested in \citet{leo12 in 1D-NLTE.}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Furthermore, \citet{lagae23 derived a much higher upper limit on C ($\rm A(C)< 5.39$, the upper limit assuming a 1D model and local thermodynamical equilibrium (1D-LTE) is added with the uncertainty assigned by the authors), making the star's transition discriminant compatible with its formation through atomic line cooling.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Since the carbon abundance determination is crucial to understanding whether the star formed through line cooling or dust cooling, we analysed newly observed high-resolution spectra that we combined with the previous observations.
elements.sent-2411-033: This allowed us to improve the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) and to improve our estimates of the upper limits of several elements, including carbon.
elements.sent-2411-034: For this investigation, we used the UVES spectra observed in the DDT programme 286.D-5045 of 2011 (7\,h observations) and the specifically requested spectra observed during programme 112.25F3.001 (22\,h observations).
elements.sent-2411-035: In programme 112.25F3.001, we observed in the setting DIC2\,437+760 (wavelength ranges 373--499 and 565--946\,nm), with slit 1\farcs{4}\ (resolving power of 40\,000).
elements.sent-2411-036: We reduced all the spectra with the ESO pipeline (on gasgano version), the option of absolute flux calibration and, for the blue-arm spectra, we used the option `noappend' in such a way as to keep all the orders separated.
elements.sent-2411-037: We corrected all the spectra for the barycentric velocity and derived the radial velocity for each observation.
elements.sent-2411-038: We shifted each spectrum for its radial velocity and merged the orders, as done for the ESPaDOnS spectra in \citet{lombardo21 .}
elements.sent-2411-039: We averaged all the spectra, and the spectrum obtained was used to derive the abundances (see Fig.\,\ref{fig:obs ).}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\hsize,clip=true]{plot_obs.pdf} \caption{Combined observed spectrum flux calibrated in the wavelength range of \ion{Ca}{ii}-K and -H. The two interstellar components are labelled as IS. The H line and few metallic lines (\ion{Fe}{i}) are visible.} \label{fig:obs}
elements.sent-2411-040: A crucial point in the analysis of the G-band to derive the carbon abundance is the order merging of the UVES spectra.

elements.sent-2411-047: This step corrects for any flux lost at the slit during the observation.

elements.sent-2411-050: The obtained spectrum is the normalised one that we compared to the complete merge spectrum, selected and normalised in the G-band wavelength range.

elements.sent-2411-055: We also computed two dedicated 3D hydrodynamical model atmospheres with the latest version of the \cobold\ code \citep[see][plus updates]{freytag2012 for stellar parameters that closely represent this star (\teff=$5773$\,K, \logg=$4.7$, [M/H]=$-4.0$, see Sec.\,\ref{sec:param ).}}
elements.sent-2411-056: The low-resolution model has $140\times 140\times 160$ grid cells, and the high-resolution models covers the same volume by $280\times 280\times 160$ cells.
elements.sent-2411-057: The frequency dependence of the radiative opacity is represented by 11 bins, each comprising opacities of similar strength.
elements.sent-2411-058: Figure\,\ref{fig:ttau depicts the temperature stratification of the resampled high-resolution model, which is almost identical to that of its low-resolution counterpart.}
elements.sent-2411-059: As is well known, the temperature stratification of the photospheres of metal-poor stars is sensitive to hydrodynamical flows that cannot be modelled in 1D.
elements.sent-2411-060: This implies a systematic photospheric temperature difference between standard 1D model atmospheres where convection is treated by the mixing-length theory and 3D hydrodynamical simulations where convective overshoot is emerging naturally.
elements.sent-2411-061: For the stellar parameters considered here, the temperature of the 3D model is cooler than predicted by the corresponding 1D model for optical depths $\log \tau_{\rm Ross < -2.0$, whereas the temperature difference is reversed in the range $-2.0 < \log \tau_{\rm Ross < -0.5$ (compare solid and dashed lines in Fig.\,\ref{fig:ttau ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: It is noteworthy that the amplitude of the temperature fluctuations in the upper photosphere is remarkably small; the 3D model can probably be represented reasonably well by its <3D> mean structure.
elements.sent-2411-063: However, we make use of the full 3D results in this work.
elements.sent-2411-064: The low amplitude of the photospheric temperature fluctuations was already recognised in the 3D model for the star used by \citet{leo12 and tentatively traced back to the damping influence of an increased specific heat caused by H$_2$ molecular formation.}
elements.sent-2411-065: As mentioned above, all chemical abundance determinations in this work (including the upper limit estimates) are based on ATLAS\,12 state-of-the-art 1D model atmospheres together with the line formation code SYNTHE \citep{k05 , used to compute synthetic spectra from the ATLAS\,12 models.}
elements.sent-2411-066: In the case of carbon only, we consider the 3D correction described in Sect.\,\ref{sec:carbon , resulting in a significantly lower C abundance.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\hsize,clip=true]{d3t57g47mm40msc11x4_tempstruct2_n4.png} \caption{Three-dimensional versus 1D temperature structure for stellar parameters \teff=$5773$\,K, \logg=$4.7$, and metallicity [M/H]=$-4.0$. The orange band outlines the 3D temperature distribution of the high-resolution model resampled to $70\times 70\times 160$ grid points. The width of the temperature distribution encountered on surfaces of equal Rosseland optical depth is indicated by the dashed blue lines enclosing 95,5\% of the data points at each height. The solid black line shows the average temperature of the 3D model, the long-dashed black line represents the $T(\tau_{\rm Ross})$ relation predicted by a 1D model atmosphere with identical stellar parameters, treating convection by standard mixing-length theory, but otherwise using the same input physics as the 3D hydrodynamical model.} \label{fig:ttau}

X, Y, and Z are Galactocentric Cartesian coordinates. Upper-right panel: $\sqrt(V_R^2+V_Z^2)$ vs $\rm V_T$ (Toomre diagram: Transversal velocity component vs a combination of the radial and vertical velocity components in Galactocentric cylindrical coordinates). The coloured points are the stars of the `'good parallax sub-sample' of \citet[][]{topos6}, plotted for reference. They are divided into thin (red) disc, thick (green) disc, and halo (blue) stars, following the \citet[][]{bensby14} criteria. elements.sent-2411-161: Fitting ranges in the G-band including an atomic line and the strongest CH features, we derived values in the range: $\rm 3.60 < A(C) < 4.23$.

elements.sent-2411-163: In the wavelength range around 429\,nm and 430\,nm, we find $\rm A(C)=4.23$, consistent with the results by \citet{FS6 in a sample of unmixed stars at a metal-poor regime ($\rm -4.2<[Fe/H]<-2$).}

elements.sent-2411-169: The upper limits that we derived for carbon are generally lower than the ones provided by \citet{lagae23 .}
elements.sent-2411-170: This is due to 1) the improved S/N of our observed spectrum in the region of the G-band, which doubled with the new observations, and 2) a different methodology that we followed: while Lagae and collaborators consider a $\chi^2$ between synthesis and observation computed over a wider wavelength range, we focused on localised strong CH features that have the chance to stand out of the noise.

elements.sent-2411-176: The comparison clearly demonstrates that the 3D lines are significantly stronger than in the 1D case.

elements.sent-2411-183: NLTE calculations for CH have only recently been conducted for a limited number of stellar parameter combinations by \citet{popa23 .}
elements.sent-2411-184: They find, in the most extreme case of a metal-poor giant, a NLTE correction of $+0.2$\,dex.
elements.sent-2411-185: We consider this an upper limit of a possible NLTE correction to be present here, since \leo\ is a dwarf where collisions play a more pronounced role, thus driving the system towards LTE.
elements.sent-2411-186: In addition, \citet{deshmukh23 find negligible deviations from chemical equilibrium of CH in the photospheres of metal-poor turn-off stars.}
elements.sent-2411-187: The higher gravity and slightly lower effective temperature of \leo\ suggest that it is unlikely that chemical equilibrium comes out differently in comparison to the situation at the turn-off.
elements.sent-2411-188: Hence, it appears rather unlikely that departures from LTE or chemical equilibrium alter the overall picture described before qualitatively.
elements.sent-2411-189: To derive the upper limit on the metallicity, Z, of the star, we applied the abundances in Table\,\ref{tab:abbo and the $3\sigma$ upper limits.}
elements.sent-2411-190: For the elements for which we have no information, we applied the solar-scaled value, except for N and O for which we applied a value scaled with the C upper limit.
elements.sent-2411-191: We derived $\rm Z<1.915\times 10^{-6}$ ($\rm Z<1.252\times 10^{-4 Z_\odot$ with $\rm Z_\odot$ from \citealt{caffausun or $\rm Z<1.378\times 10^{-4 Z_\odot$ with $\rm Z_\odot$ from \citealt{asplund21 ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-192: This value relies on the 1D-LTE abundances.
elements.sent-2411-193: The NLTE corrections would increase the metallicity, but only of the elements that represent a minority in the stellar photosphere: an increase by 0.5\,dex in Fe would bring the Z upper limit to $1.970\times 10^{-6}$.
elements.sent-2411-194: However, if corrections have to be applied, the 3D corrections for C should be taken into account, reducing the stellar metallicity by a larger amount, because carbon is much more abundant in the stellar photosphere than iron.
elements.sent-2411-195: By applying the NLTE correction from Table\,\ref{tab:abbo and the 3D correction on C, we derived $\rm Z<1.660\times 10^{-6}$.}
elements.sent-2411-196: With the 1D upper limit $\rm [C/H]<-3.82$ and adopting the scaled value for oxygen, for this star, we derived $\rm D<-3.71$.
elements.sent-2411-197: By applying the 3D correction on the C abundance and leaving the [O/H] upper limit unchanged, we derived $\rm D<-4.23$.
elements.sent-2411-198: These values suggest that the star was formed through dust cooling, as suggested by several authors \citep[see][]{schneider12,klessen12,chiaki14,bovino16 , but the fragmentation scenario presented by \citet{greif10 is also a possibility for the formation of this star.}}
elements.sent-2411-199: Few stars with $\rm [Fe/H]<-4.5$\,dex are known, and just two stars \citep[this star and Pristine\,J221.8781+09.7844][]{starkenburg18 do not have a measurable G-band, putting them outside the CEMP class that hosts all the other stars known in this iron-abundance range.}
elements.sent-2411-200: Pristine\,J221.8781+09.7844 has a loose [C/Fe] upper limit \citep[2.3 dex in][with S/N=90 at 400\,nm]{lardo21 , but the G-band is not visible in its spectrum either.}
elements.sent-2411-201: The lower the [Fe/H] ratio, the more difficult is to reach a $\rm [C/Fe]<1$\,dex upper limit, according to the definition of CEMP stars in \citet{beers05 .}
elements.sent-2411-202: Looking at the ultra iron-poor stars in \citet{topos2 , the low carbon band is around $\rm A(C)\sim 6.7$, such that $\rm A(C)< 5.5$ could be a better definition for a star not belonging to the CEMP class in the case that it has $\rm [Fe/H]<-4.5$.}
elements.sent-2411-203: This is a tentative Li detection providing $\rm A(Li)=1.08$ (see Table\,\ref{tab:abbo ), a value well below the Spite plateau \citep{spite82 .}}
elements.sent-2411-204: In any case, whether with a low Li abundance or entirely free of Li, the star could have destroyed the Li during its life (e.g. if it were a blue straggler) or have formed from a gas cloud already depleted in Li.
elements.sent-2411-205: Also, HE\,0107\,5240 \citep{christlieb02 has less lithium than expected from its parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-206: In fact, $\rm A(Li)<0.50$ \citep[see][]{aguado22 , and the star should still have $\rm A(Li)\sim 1$ \citep{mucciarelli22 .}}
elements.sent-2411-207: This can be an indication that the ultra Fe-poor stars formed from a cloud depleted in Li.
elements.sent-2411-208: But, to add complexity to the picture, we recall that at the ultra Fe-poor regime, there are also stars with the Li abundance on the Spite plateau \citep[e.g. SDSS\,J002314.00+030758.0][]{aguado19 or that were on the Spite plateau before diluting the Li following the first dredge-up \citep[e.g. SMSS\,J031300.36-670839.3][]{keller14 .}}
elements.sent-2411-209: The relatively low contents of Al and Na are still compatible with extremely metal-poor (EMP) stars \citep[see][]{cayrel04 .}
elements.sent-2411-210: The stringent Sr upper limit puts the star as not rich in heavy elements but it is still consistent with the high-quality EMP sample investigated by \citet{francois07 .}
elements.sent-2411-211: The star is in a pro-grade disc orbit, confined in the Galactic disc.
elements.sent-2411-212: Due to the poor metal content of this star, \citet[][see also references therein]{klessen23 suggest that it formed through dust cooling.}
elements.sent-2411-213: The two considerations, that 1) the star is in a Galactic disc orbit and 2) its photospheric metal content is low, may invite to consider \leo\ as a Pop\,III star, polluted by the interstellar gas during its long (probably more than 13\,Gyr) journey through the Galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-214: The number of encountered clouds is expected to be large due to the long life of this star and the higher number of gas clouds on the Galactic disc than on the Galactic halo; the relatively low [Mg/Fe] and [Ti/Fe] ratios in \leo\ are consistent with the pollution expected by \citet{johnson15 in a main-sequence Pop\,III star in the case of $\rm \rho_{grain =3 g cm^{-3}$, but, in this case, the expected [Si/Fe] would be expected to be lower by about 0.3\,dex.}}
elements.sent-2411-215: Our investigation confirms \leo\ to be the most metal poor object known to date and does not support any enhancement in carbon.
elements.sent-2411-216: This leaves open the routes of dust cooling or fragmentation to explain its formation.
